metadata.notes: National average installed costs. Apply regional_adjustments multipliers by state for location-adjusted costs. Costs are full replacement unless noted. Package costs are sums of component upgrade costs.

sources_summary.regional: RSMeans city cost indices via Scout loc_cost_adj.csv
gap_fill: EIA Updated Buildings Sector Equipment Costs 2023
Industry estimates for demand flexibility and window film

1.upgrade_name: Variable Speed HP RTU, Electric Backup

sources.title: Buildings Annual Technology Baseline. NREL, forthcoming

1.notes: Variable speed HP RTU, electric backup
1.description: Replaces existing packaged rooftop units with variable-speed heat pump RTUs with electric resistance backup heating. Provides efficient heating and cooling with variable airflow for improved part-load performance.

2.upgrade_name: Variable Speed HP RTU, Original Heating Fuel Backup

sources.title: Buildings Annual Technology Baseline. NREL, forthcoming

2.notes: Variable speed HP RTU, original fuel backup
2.description: Replaces existing packaged rooftop units with variable-speed heat pump RTUs that retain the original heating fuel as backup. The heat pump serves as the primary heating source with fossil fuel backup for extreme cold conditions.

3.upgrade_name: Variable Speed HP RTU, Electric Backup, Energy Recovery

sources.title: Buildings Annual Technology Baseline. NREL, forthcoming

3.notes: Variable speed HP RTU + energy recovery; cost does not include ERV adder
3.description: Replaces existing packaged rooftop units with variable-speed heat pump RTUs with electric backup and adds energy recovery ventilators (ERVs) to precondition outdoor air using exhaust air energy.

4.upgrade_name: Standard Performance HP RTU, Electric Backup

sources.title: Buildings Annual Technology Baseline. NREL, forthcoming

4.description: Replaces existing packaged rooftop units with standard-performance heat pump RTUs (lower efficiency than variable-speed) with electric resistance backup heating.

5.upgrade_name: Standard Performance HP RTU using Lab Data, Electric Backup

sources.title: Buildings Annual Technology Baseline. NREL, forthcoming

5.notes: Standard performance HP RTU using lab data
5.description: Replaces existing packaged rooftop units with standard-performance heat pump RTUs sized using laboratory test data, with electric resistance backup heating. Performance curves derived from controlled lab measurements.

6.upgrade_name: Standard Performance HP RTU, Electric Backup + Roof Insulation

sources.title: Buildings Annual Technology Baseline. NREL, forthcoming

6.notes: HP RTU + roof insulation; cost is HP RTU portion only, add roof insulation cost (upgrade 49)
6.description: Combines a standard-performance heat pump RTU replacement with electric backup and addition of roof insulation to meet current code requirements. Reduces both HVAC and envelope losses.

7.upgrade_name: Standard Performance HP RTU, Electric Backup + New Windows

sources.title: Buildings Annual Technology Baseline. NREL, forthcoming

7.notes: HP RTU + new windows; cost is HP RTU portion only, add window cost (upgrade 52)
7.description: Combines a standard-performance heat pump RTU replacement with electric backup and installation of new high-performance windows. Addresses both HVAC efficiency and solar/thermal gains through glazing.

8.upgrade_name: Standard Performance HP RTU, Electric Backup, 32F Minimum Compressor Lockout

sources.title: Buildings Annual Technology Baseline. NREL, forthcoming

8.notes: Standard HP RTU with 32F compressor lockout
8.description: Replaces existing rooftop units with standard-performance heat pump RTUs with electric backup, configured with a 32°F minimum compressor lockout temperature to limit heat pump operation in very cold conditions.

9.upgrade_name: Standard Performance HP RTU, Electric Backup, 2F Unoccupied Htg Thermostat Setback

sources.title: Buildings Annual Technology Baseline. NREL, forthcoming

9.notes: Standard HP RTU with 2F unoccupied setback
9.description: Replaces existing rooftop units with standard-performance heat pump RTUs with electric backup, adding a 2°F thermostat setback during unoccupied heating periods to reduce energy use.

10.upgrade_name: Commercial HVAC Challenge HP RTU, Electric Backup

sources.title: Buildings Annual Technology Baseline. NREL, forthcoming

10.notes: Commercial HVAC Challenge HP RTU, high performance
10.description: Replaces existing rooftop units with high-performance heat pump RTUs meeting the DOE Commercial HVAC Challenge specifications, with electric resistance backup heating. Targets best-in-class efficiency levels.

sources.title: Buildings Annual Technology Baseline. NREL, forthcoming

11.notes: Advanced RTU controls; cost is baseline RTU (controls upgrade is incremental)
11.description: Upgrades existing rooftop units with advanced features including variable-speed fans, improved economizer controls, and optimized staging to improve part-load efficiency without full equipment replacement.

sources.name: EIA Updated Buildings Sector Equipment Costs 2023

12.notes: VRF with DOAS. EIA commercial HVAC tables, VRF heat recovery systems.
12.description: Replaces existing HVAC systems with variable refrigerant flow (VRF) systems paired with a dedicated outdoor air system (DOAS) for ventilation. VRF provides zonal heating and cooling with heat recovery capability.

sources.name: EIA Updated Buildings Sector Equipment Costs 2023

13.notes: VRF with 25% upsizing allowance, higher cost for oversized equipment.
13.description: Replaces existing HVAC systems with variable refrigerant flow (VRF) systems sized with a 25% upsizing allowance to ensure adequate capacity in extreme conditions and account for sizing uncertainty.

sources.name: EIA Updated Buildings Sector Equipment Costs 2023

14.notes: DOAS with HP minisplits. EIA ductless/mini-split HP tables.
14.description: Replaces existing HVAC systems with ductless heat pump mini-splits served by a dedicated outdoor air system (DOAS) for ventilation. Provides high-efficiency zonal heating and cooling without ductwork losses.

sources.title: Buildings Annual Technology Baseline. NREL, forthcoming

15.description: Replaces existing hot water boilers with air-to-water heat pump boilers with electric resistance backup. Serves hydronic heating systems with significantly improved efficiency over fossil fuel boilers.

sources.title: Buildings Annual Technology Baseline. NREL, forthcoming

16.description: Replaces existing hot water boilers with air-to-water heat pump boilers that retain natural gas backup for peak heating loads. The heat pump operates as the primary heating source.

sources.title: Updated Buildings Sector Appliance and Equipment Costs and Efficiencies
sources.author: U.S. Energy Information Administration

17.notes: Condensing gas boiler; using gas_boiler component cost
17.description: Replaces existing gas-fired boilers with condensing natural gas boilers achieving 95%+ thermal efficiency by recovering latent heat from flue gases.

18.scout_ecm_name: (C) Ref. Case Elec. Boiler FS (NG Boiler)

sources.title: Buildings Annual Technology Baseline. NREL, forthcoming

18.description: Replaces existing boilers with electric resistance boilers for buildings transitioning to all-electric heating. Provides 100% conversion efficiency but higher operating costs than heat pump alternatives.

19.notes: Air side economizers for AHUs. PNNL commercial HVAC controls cost per sqft.
19.description: Adds air-side economizers to existing air handling units to provide free cooling using outdoor air when ambient conditions are favorable, reducing mechanical cooling energy.

20.notes: Demand control ventilation. CO2 sensors + controls.
20.description: Installs CO2-based demand control ventilation (DCV) sensors and controls on air handling units to modulate outdoor air intake based on actual occupancy, reducing ventilation energy during partial occupancy.

21.notes: Energy recovery ventilator for AHUs. Includes enthalpy wheel or plate HX.
21.description: Adds energy recovery ventilators (ERVs) to existing air handling units to transfer heat and moisture between exhaust and incoming outdoor air streams, reducing heating and cooling loads from ventilation.

22.notes: Advanced RTU controls. Retrofit controller add-on.
22.description: Upgrades existing rooftop unit controls with advanced strategies including integrated economizer operation, demand-based ventilation, optimal start/stop, and supply air temperature reset.

23.notes: Unoccupied AHU control. Schedule programming + occupancy sensors.
23.description: Implements automated shutdown of air handling units during unoccupied periods with optimized morning warm-up/cool-down sequences, reducing fan energy and conditioning loads outside operating hours.

24.upgrade_name: Fan Static Pressure Reset for Multizone VAV
24.notes: Fan static pressure reset for multizone VAV. Controls upgrade.
24.description: Adds static pressure reset controls to multizone variable air volume (VAV) systems, allowing supply fan speed to modulate based on zone demand rather than maintaining constant duct pressure.

25.notes: Thermostat setbacks. Programmable thermostat or BMS schedule change.
25.description: Implements nighttime and weekend thermostat setbacks to reduce heating and cooling energy during unoccupied hours. Heating setpoints are lowered and cooling setpoints raised during setback periods.

26.notes: VFD pumps. Variable frequency drive retrofit on hydronic pumps.
26.description: Replaces constant-speed hydronic pumps with variable frequency drive (VFD) equipped pumps that modulate flow based on system demand, significantly reducing pumping energy at part-load conditions.

27.notes: Ideal thermal air loads — theoretical max savings, not a real measure.
27.description: Theoretical measure representing ideal HVAC performance with zero distribution losses, used as a benchmarking reference for maximum achievable HVAC efficiency.

sources.title: Buildings Annual Technology Baseline. NREL, forthcoming

28.notes: Hydronic water-to-water GHP. Includes bore field costs.
28.description: Replaces existing hydronic heating and cooling systems with water-to-water geothermal heat pumps using vertical closed-loop ground heat exchangers. Serves existing hydronic distribution piping.

sources.title: Buildings Annual Technology Baseline. NREL, forthcoming

29.description: Replaces existing HVAC systems with packaged water-to-air geothermal heat pump units connected to vertical closed-loop ground heat exchangers. Each unit provides direct air heating and cooling.

sources.title: Buildings Annual Technology Baseline. NREL, forthcoming

30.description: Replaces existing HVAC systems with console-style water-to-air geothermal heat pump units connected to vertical closed-loop ground heat exchangers. Console units are wall-mounted for perimeter zone conditioning.

sources.title: Updated Buildings Sector Appliance and Equipment Costs and Efficiencies
sources.author: U.S. Energy Information Administration

31.notes: Chiller replacement; using chiller component costs from Scout ECM.
31.description: Replaces existing chillers with high-efficiency chillers meeting current ASHRAE 90.1 minimum efficiency requirements, improving cooling plant performance.

32.upgrade_name: Demand Flexibility, Thermostat Control, Load Shed for Daily Bldg Peak Reduction
32.notes: Controls-only measure. Cost = BMS programming + smart thermostat/lighting controls.
32.description: Implements thermostat-based demand flexibility to shed HVAC load during daily building peak periods by adjusting temperature setpoints, reducing the building's peak demand.

33.upgrade_name: Demand Flexibility, Thermostat Control, Load Shift for Daily Bldg Peak Reduction
33.notes: Controls-only measure. Cost = BMS programming + smart thermostat/lighting controls.
33.description: Implements thermostat-based demand flexibility to shift HVAC load away from daily building peak periods through pre-conditioning (pre-cooling or pre-heating) before peak hours.

34.upgrade_name: Demand Flexibility, Lighting Control, Load Shed for Daily Bldg Peak Reduction
34.notes: Controls-only measure. Cost = BMS programming + smart thermostat/lighting controls.
34.description: Implements lighting-based demand flexibility by shedding (dimming or switching off) non-essential lighting during daily building peak demand periods.

35.upgrade_name: Demand Flexibility, Lighting Control, Load Shed for Daily GHG Emission Reduction
35.notes: Controls-only measure. Cost = BMS programming + smart thermostat/lighting controls.
35.description: Implements lighting-based demand flexibility by shedding non-essential lighting during periods of high grid greenhouse gas emissions to reduce the building's carbon impact.

36.upgrade_name: Demand Flexibility, Thermostat Control, Load Shed for Grid Peak Reduction
36.notes: Controls-only measure. Cost = BMS programming + smart thermostat/lighting controls.
36.description: Implements thermostat-based demand flexibility to shed HVAC load during grid-level peak demand periods by adjusting temperature setpoints to reduce strain on the electric grid.

37.upgrade_name: Demand Flexibility, Lighting Control, Load Shed for Grid Peak Reduction
37.notes: Controls-only measure. Cost = BMS programming + smart thermostat/lighting controls.
37.description: Implements lighting-based demand flexibility by shedding non-essential lighting during grid-level peak demand periods to reduce strain on the electric grid.

38.upgrade_name: Demand Flexibility, Plug Load Control (GEB Gem), Load Shed for Grid Peak Reduction
38.notes: Controls-only measure. Cost = BMS programming + smart thermostat/lighting controls.
38.description: Implements plug load demand flexibility using GEB Gem controls to shed non-essential plug loads during grid-level peak demand periods.

39.upgrade_name: Demand Flexibility, Lighting Control (GEB Gem), Load Shed for Grid Peak Reduction
39.notes: Controls-only measure. Cost = BMS programming + smart thermostat/lighting controls.
39.description: Implements lighting demand flexibility using GEB Gem controls to shed non-essential lighting during grid-level peak demand periods with optimized dimming strategies.

40.upgrade_name: Demand Flexibility, Thermostat Control (GEB Gem), Load Shed for Grid Peak Reduction
40.notes: Controls-only measure. Cost = BMS programming + smart thermostat/lighting controls.
40.description: Implements thermostat demand flexibility using GEB Gem controls to shed HVAC load during grid-level peak demand periods with optimized setpoint adjustment strategies.

41.upgrade_name: Demand Flexibility, Preheat (GEB Gem), Load Shift for Grid Peak Reduction
41.notes: Controls-only measure. Cost = BMS programming + smart thermostat/lighting controls.
41.description: Implements GEB Gem-controlled pre-heating load shifting to move heating energy consumption ahead of grid peak periods, reducing demand during peak hours.

42.upgrade_name: Demand Flexibility, Precool (GEB Gem), Load Shift for Grid Peak Reduction
42.notes: Controls-only measure. Cost = BMS programming + smart thermostat/lighting controls.
42.description: Implements GEB Gem-controlled pre-cooling load shifting to move cooling energy consumption ahead of grid peak periods, reducing demand during peak hours.

sources.title: National Cost:Effectiveness of ANSI/ASHRAE/IES Standard 90.1:2019

43.description: Retrofits all applicable interior and exterior lighting fixtures to LED technology, reducing lighting energy consumption by 30-60% compared to fluorescent and HID fixtures.

45.description: Replaces gas-fired commercial kitchen equipment (ovens, ranges, fryers, griddles) with high-efficiency electric equivalents including induction cooktops and electric convection ovens.

19.upgrade_name: Electric Vehicle Adoption with Level 1 Charging
19.notes: EV Level 1 charging. Charger hardware only, vehicle cost excluded.
19.description: Models household adoption of an electric vehicle charged with Level 1 (120V, ~1.4 kW) home charging, adding EV electricity consumption to the dwelling's energy profile.

20.upgrade_name: Electric Vehicle Adoption with Level 2 Charging
20.notes: EV Level 2 charging. Includes 240V wiring and charger.
20.description: Models household adoption of an electric vehicle charged with Level 2 (240V, ~7.7 kW) home charging, adding EV electricity consumption to the dwelling's energy profile.

21.upgrade_name: Efficient Electric Vehicle Adoption with Level 2 Charging
21.notes: Efficient EV with L2 charging. Same charger cost, vehicle cost excluded.
21.description: Models household adoption of a high-efficiency electric vehicle (lower kWh/mile) charged with Level 2 (240V) home charging, representing best-in-class EV efficiency.

22.upgrade_name: Electric Vehicle Adoption with Level 2 Charging and Demand Flexibility

sources.name: NREL REMDB 2024 — EV Charger + Smart Thermostat

22.notes: EV L2 charging + demand flexibility. Charger + smart thermostat.
22.description: Models electric vehicle adoption with Level 2 home charging and demand-flexible charging controls that shift charging to off-peak grid periods to reduce peak demand impacts.

23.upgrade_name: Efficient Electric Vehicle Adoption with Level 2 Charging and Demand Flexibility

sources.name: NREL REMDB 2024 — EV Charger + Smart Thermostat

23.notes: Efficient EV L2 + demand flexibility. Same charger cost.
23.description: Models high-efficiency electric vehicle adoption with Level 2 home charging and demand-flexible charging controls that shift charging to off-peak grid periods.

24.upgrade_name: HVAC Demand Flexibility - On-peak Load Shedding, 2F Offset
24.notes: HVAC demand flexibility via smart thermostat. Controls-only measure.
24.description: Implements HVAC demand flexibility by shedding load during on-peak periods with a 2°F thermostat offset (raising cooling setpoint or lowering heating setpoint) to reduce peak electricity demand.

25.upgrade_name: HVAC Demand Flexibility - Pre-peak Load Shifting, 2F Offset, 4hr Pre-peak Duration
25.notes: HVAC demand flexibility via smart thermostat. Controls-only measure.
25.description: Implements HVAC demand flexibility by pre-conditioning the home (pre-cooling or pre-heating) with a 2°F offset during a 4-hour window before peak periods, then allowing temperature to drift during peak.

26.upgrade_name: HVAC Demand Flexibility - On-peak Load Shedding, 4F Offset
26.notes: HVAC demand flexibility via smart thermostat. Controls-only measure.
26.description: Implements HVAC demand flexibility by shedding load during on-peak periods with a 4°F thermostat offset for more aggressive peak demand reduction compared to the 2°F variant.

27.upgrade_name: HVAC Demand Flexibility - Pre-peak Load Shifting, 4F Offset, 4hr Pre-peak Duration
27.notes: HVAC demand flexibility via smart thermostat. Controls-only measure.
27.description: Implements HVAC demand flexibility by pre-conditioning the home with a 4°F offset during a 4-hour window before peak periods, then allowing temperature to drift during peak for greater load shifting.

28.upgrade_name: HVAC Demand Flexibility - Pre-peak Load Shifting, 2F Offset, 1hr Pre-peak Duration
28.notes: HVAC demand flexibility via smart thermostat. Controls-only measure.
28.description: Implements HVAC demand flexibility by pre-conditioning the home with a 2°F offset during a shorter 1-hour window before peak periods, providing moderate load shifting with minimal pre-peak energy increase.

sources.title: Opaque Envelopes: Pathway to Building Energy Efficiency and Demand Flexibility RDO Report
sources.author: U.S. Department of Energy, Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy, Building Technologies Office

29.description: Performs general air sealing to reduce building infiltration rates, targeting accessible air leakage pathways throughout the dwelling envelope.

30.upgrade_name: General Air Sealing, Attic Floor Insulation, and Duct Sealing
30.notes: General Air Sealing + Attic Insulation + Duct Sealing
component_details: [12] Attic Floor Insulation for Unfinished Attics
30.description: Combines general air sealing with attic floor insulation and duct sealing and insulation for a cost-effective envelope improvement package addressing major infiltration and insulation deficiencies.
